is a [highway](/wiki/Autoroute_(Quebec) "Autoroute (Quebec)") in western [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), Canada. It is also called the **Décarie Expressway** (English) or ***Autoroute Décarie*** (French) between the [Turcot](/wiki/Turcot_Interchange "Turcot Interchange") and [Décarie](/wiki/D%C3%A9carie_Interchange "Décarie Interchange") Interchanges in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") and the **Laurentian Autoroute** (English) or ***Autoroute des Laurentides*** (French) north of [Autoroute 40](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_40 "Quebec Autoroute 40"). It was, until the extension of [Autoroute 25](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_25 "Quebec Autoroute 25") was opened in 2011, the only constructed north-south autoroute to go out of Montreal on both sides. A-15 begins at the end of [Interstate 87](/wiki/Interstate_87_(New_York) "Interstate 87 (New York)") at the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") border at [Saint-Bernard-de-Lacolle](/wiki/Saint-Bernard-de-Lacolle,_Quebec "Saint-Bernard-de-Lacolle, Quebec") and extends via Montreal to [Sainte-Agathe-des-Monts](/wiki/Sainte-Agathe-des-Monts "Sainte-Agathe-des-Monts") with an eventual continuation beyond [Mont-Tremblant](/wiki/Mont-Tremblant,_Quebec "Mont-Tremblant, Quebec"). The total length of A-15 is 164 km (101.9 mi), including a short concurrency (4 km or 2.5 mi) with Autoroute 40 (Boulevard/Autoroute Métropolitan) that connects the two main sections.

**Autoroute 15** is a [highway](/wiki/Autoroute_(Quebec) "Autoroute (Quebec)") in western [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), Canada. It is also called the **Décarie Expressway** (English) or ***Autoroute Décarie*** (French) between the [Turcot](/wiki/Turcot_Interchange "Turcot Interchange") and [Décarie](/wiki/D%C3%A9carie_Interchange "Décarie Interchange") Interchanges in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") and the **Laurentian Autoroute** (English) or ***Autoroute des Laurentides*** (French) north of [Autoroute 40](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_40 "Quebec Autoroute 40"). It was, until the extension of [Autoroute 25](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_25 "Quebec Autoroute 25") was opened in 2011, the only constructed north-south autoroute to go out of Montreal on both sides. A-15 begins at the end of [Interstate 87](/wiki/Interstate_87_(New_York) "Interstate 87 (New York)") at the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") border at [Saint-Bernard-de-Lacolle](/wiki/Saint-Bernard-de-Lacolle,_Quebec "Saint-Bernard-de-Lacolle, Quebec") and extends via Montreal to [Sainte-Agathe-des-Monts](/wiki/Sainte-Agathe-des-Monts "Sainte-Agathe-des-Monts") with an eventual continuation beyond [Mont-Tremblant](/wiki/Mont-Tremblant,_Quebec "Mont-Tremblant, Quebec"). The total length of A-15 is 164 km (101.9 mi), including a short concurrency (4 km or 2.5 mi) with Autoroute 40 (Boulevard/Autoroute Métropolitan) that connects the two main sections.

## Road description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quebec_Autoroute_15&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Road description")]

### Southern section

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quebec_Autoroute_15&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Southern section")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Montreal_autoroute.jpg/250px-Montreal_autoroute.jpg)](/wiki/File:Montreal_autoroute.jpg)

Autoroute 15 in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), facing southwards at the [Autoroute 20](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_20 "Quebec Autoroute 20") junction (Exit 63)

The southern section of A-15 connects the [south shore](/wiki/South_Shore_(Montreal) "South Shore (Montreal)") suburbs of [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") and is also the primary trade corridor route between Montreal and [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") linking Quebec Autoroute 15 to [Interstate 87](/wiki/Interstate_87_(New_York) "Interstate 87 (New York)") at the Canada-United States border at the [Champlain-St. Bernard de Lacolle Border Crossing](/wiki/Champlain-St._Bernard_de_Lacolle_Border_Crossing "Champlain-St. Bernard de Lacolle Border Crossing"). This was the former [Route 9](/wiki/Quebec_Route_9 "Quebec Route 9"), and connected with [US 9](/wiki/U.S._Route_9_in_New_York "U.S. Route 9 in New York") on the western shore of [Lake Champlain](/wiki/Lake_Champlain "Lake Champlain"). In [Brossard](/wiki/Brossard,_Quebec "Brossard, Quebec"), it joins up with [A-10](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_10 "Quebec Autoroute 10") and [A-20](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_20 "Quebec Autoroute 20") across the [Champlain Bridge](/wiki/Champlain_Bridge_(Montreal) "Champlain Bridge (Montreal)") into Montreal. The A-10 splits off almost immediately after crossing the bridge to head into downtown Montreal at the [Bonaventure Expressway](/wiki/Bonaventure_Expressway "Bonaventure Expressway") and the A-20 splits off shortly after at the [Turcot Interchange](/wiki/Turcot_Interchange "Turcot Interchange") (*échangeur Turcot*), leaving the A-15 to continue northward as Autoroute Décarie until the [Décarie Interchange](/wiki/D%C3%A9carie_Interchange "Décarie Interchange") (*échangeur Décarie*) with the A-40 at the point where it turns from the [Trans-Canada](/wiki/Trans-Canada "Trans-Canada") into the Metropolitan Expressway.

The route is also connected to [Autoroute 30](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_30 "Quebec Autoroute 30") in [Candiac](/wiki/Candiac,_Quebec "Candiac, Quebec") which was completed to [Autoroute 20](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_20 "Quebec Autoroute 20") in 2012 providing quick access to the south shore of Montreal, to southern communities located alongside Autoroute 15 and to the [Canada–US border](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_border "Canada–United States border") in [Lacolle](/wiki/Lacolle,_Quebec "Lacolle, Quebec"). It will also give quicker access from there to areas west of Montreal and also [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa") and [Gatineau](/wiki/Gatineau "Gatineau").

#### Décarie Autoroute

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quebec_Autoroute_15&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Décarie Autoroute")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Autoroute_D%C3%A9carie.jpg/330px-Autoroute_D%C3%A9carie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Autoroute_D%C3%A9carie.jpg)

Décarie Highway (North) at the height of Queen Mary Road

The Décarie Autoroute is a [sunken highway](/wiki/Trench#Civil_engineering "Trench") between the northbound and southbound lanes of Decarie Boulevard from the [Metropolitan Autoroute](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_40 "Quebec Autoroute 40") at its northern end to [Monkland Avenue](/wiki/Monkland_Avenue_(Montreal) "Monkland Avenue (Montreal)") and the [Villa Maria Metro station](/wiki/Villa-Maria_station_(Montreal_Metro) "Villa-Maria station (Montreal Metro)") at its southern end. It was built on a wide expanse of vacant land, donated to the city by the Décarie estate on the condition that a streetcar line would be established. The decommissioning of the streetcar system in 1959 left the right-of-way as an obvious choice for a highway and so the Décarie Autoroute was dug there. South of [Queen Mary Road](/wiki/Queen_Mary_Road_(Montreal) "Queen Mary Road (Montreal)"), however, a significant number of houses were demolished.

To avoid demolishing Notre-Dame-de-Grâce Church, the highway makes a slight westerly jog below [Côte-Saint-Luc Road](/wiki/C%C3%B4te-Saint-Luc_Road "Côte-Saint-Luc Road") and runs through a short [tunnel](/wiki/Tunnel "Tunnel"), before emerging between Addington and Botrel Streets and running down to [Sherbrooke Street](/wiki/Sherbrooke_Street "Sherbrooke Street") and [Saint Jacques Street](/wiki/Saint_Jacques_Street "Saint Jacques Street"), where it spectacularly goes from being below the ground to well above the ground as it intersects with [Autoroute 20](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_20 "Quebec Autoroute 20") and [Route 136](/wiki/Quebec_Route_136_(Montreal) "Quebec Route 136 (Montreal)") in the infamous [Turcot Interchange](/wiki/Turcot_Interchange "Turcot Interchange") (dubbed "[Spaghetti Junction](/wiki/Spaghetti_Junction "Spaghetti Junction")" by train crews operating the former CN Rail Turcot Yard). Following the conversion from streetcar line to the highway, the Décarie estate sued the city but was unable to prevail because it did not document its case well enough for the nevertheless sympathetic court.

#### Décarie Boulevard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quebec_Autoroute_15&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Décarie Boulevard")]

**Décarie Boulevard** itself continues; from [Monkland Avenue](/wiki/Monkland_Village#Monkland_Avenue "Monkland Village") south to Saint Jacques Street past the [McGill University Health Centre](/wiki/McGill_University_Health_Centre "McGill University Health Centre") Glen Campus superhospital; and from [Autoroute 40](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_40 "Quebec Autoroute 40") north into [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") past [Du College station](/wiki/Du_College_station "Du College station") and [Côte-Vertu station](/wiki/C%C3%B4te-Vertu_station "Côte-Vertu station")/[Norgate shopping centre](/wiki/Norgate_shopping_centre "Norgate shopping centre") to Poirier Street. Between Monkland Avenue and [A 40](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_40 "Quebec Autoroute 40"), Décarie Boulevard serves as sort of a service road on both sides of the autoroute.

### Northern section

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quebec_Autoroute_15&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Northern section")]

After its concurrency with [A-40](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_40 "Quebec Autoroute 40"), the northern section of A-15 is the main highway route to the [Laurentian Mountains](/wiki/Laurentian_Mountains "Laurentian Mountains") or [Laurentides](/wiki/Laurentides "Laurentides"), until it downgrades to [Route 117](/wiki/Quebec_Route_117 "Quebec Route 117"). It also links up to the northern suburbs of Montreal, as well as provides a connection to the [A-440](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_440_(Laval) "Quebec Autoroute 440 (Laval)"), [A-640](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_640 "Quebec Autoroute 640") and the [A-50](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_50 "Quebec Autoroute 50") in [Mirabel](/wiki/Mirabel,_Quebec "Mirabel, Quebec"). The first section from A-40 to [Saint-Jérôme](/wiki/Saint-J%C3%A9r%C3%B4me,_Quebec "Saint-Jérôme, Quebec") was opened on August 29, 1959 (source Montreal Star Aug. 29, 1959, page 3) as a toll road, although the tolls were removed in 1985. This section was also the first to be designed as an autoroute in the province. It was named Autoroute Montréal-Laurentides during the 1960s.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Over the next years, it was extended north to Sainte-Agathe-des-Monts as a new connection to touristic and skiing destinations in the Laurentides including in [Saint-Sauveur](/wiki/Saint-Sauveur,_Quebec "Saint-Sauveur, Quebec"), [Sainte-Adèle](/wiki/Sainte-Ad%C3%A8le,_Quebec "Sainte-Adèle, Quebec") and [Estérel](/wiki/Est%C3%A9rel,_Quebec "Estérel, Quebec"). In the future, it is possible that the A-15 may continue even farther north, past [Mont-Tremblant](/wiki/Mont-Tremblant "Mont-Tremblant"), as Route 117 is already an at-grade expressway with a freeway bypass of Mont-Tremblant completed, and the name *Autoroute des Laurentides* is also recognized on the freeway bypass (and exit numbers continue). This section is numbered separately from the southern section as if it were a different route. The northern route is also part of the [Trans-Canada Highway](/wiki/Trans-Canada_Highway "Trans-Canada Highway").

## Disasters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quebec_Autoroute_15&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Disasters")]

On February 19, 2020, white-out conditions caused a pile-up involving more than 200 vehicles in [La Prairie](/wiki/La_Prairie,_Quebec "La Prairie, Quebec"), a suburb of [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal").[[5]](#cite_note-6) Two people died and more than 70 were injured.

On June 18, 2000, the southern portion of the [Boulevard du Souvenir](/wiki/2000_Boulevard_du_Souvenir_overpass_collapse "2000 Boulevard du Souvenir overpass collapse") overpass in Laval (which crosses over Quebec Autoroute 15), under reconstruction, collapsed into the roadway, killing one and injuring two when cars were crushed underneath the structure. Sixteen beams weighing about 70 tonnes (69 long tons; 77 short tons) each fell. The contractor was faulted for shoddy work. The arched [concrete](/wiki/Concrete "Concrete") [beams](/wiki/Beam_(structure) "Beam (structure)") were unsecured and tipped over like [dominoes](/wiki/Dominoes "Dominoes"), many of them breaking into pieces.

The expressway has also seen flooding. On July 14, 1987, a sudden torrential downpour caused by an HP [supercell](/wiki/Supercell "Supercell") [thunderstorm](/wiki/Thunderstorm "Thunderstorm") dumped over 100 millimetres (3.9 inches) of rain in just over one hour across the city. The Décarie Expressway, which is below-grade, was heavily flooded and became a river. At some locations, the water reached a maximum of 3.6 metres (12 feet) in depth on the roadway. Over 300 vehicles were abandoned when they were submerged. Two people were killed by the storm.[[6]](#cite_note-7) One 80-year-old man on the Expressway drowned and another one was killed by [electrical wires](/wiki/Overhead_power_line "Overhead power line") ([electrocuted](/wiki/Electrocution "Electrocution")). On July 5, 2005, another torrential downpour flooded portions of the Expressway after several manhole covers blew open from the storm sewers below them being overloaded.[[7]](#cite_note-8)
